text: We present a case of a robotic resection of abdominal wall metastasis from colon cancer primary.
The patient is a fifty-eight year old male diagnosed with an abdominal wall recurrence of colorectal primary after initial laparoscopic sigmoidectomy and adjuvant chemotherapy.
Past medical history includes coronary artery disease with stents, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and gastroesophageal reflux disease.
Past surgical history, laparoscopic sigmoidectomy with end colostomy, total left hepatic lobectomy, and partial right hepatectomy, and a robotic-assisted colostomy closure.
Medications include aspirin and ranitidine.
Abdominal wall metastases from colorectal primaries are rare, with a reported incidence of one to two percent in both open and laparoscopic colectomies.
Multiple theories have been postulated for their occurrence. These include tumor invasion, lymphatic spread, and cancer cell seeding.
Recent literature suggests tumor manipulation during surgery is the predominant factor for tumor seeding. Aerosolization associated with pneumoperitoneum is thought to play a smaller secondary role.
The PET CT scan reveals a 2.1 by 1.8 centimeter hypermetabolic lesion located within the left rectus musculature, indicating an abdominal wall recurrence.
This was confirmed as adenocarcinoma consistent with colorectal primary on corneal biopsy.
The patient was scheduled for elective robotic assisted resection of abdominal wall metastasis.
The patient is positioned supine with arms tucked, and an optical trocar is inserted in the left upper quadrant for the robotic approach.
Two additional 8mm trocars are placed in the supraumbilical region and the right lower quadrant, visible on the patient's abdomen.
The surgeon identifies the abdominal wall metastasis located at the medial portion of the patient's previous colostomy closure site, with no visible evidence of peritoneal metastasis.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to score the mass, initiating careful circumferential excision of the abdominal wall metastasis.
The surgeon applies traction and counter traction to maintain the correct dissection plane while continuing to excise the abdominal wall metastasis using robotic assistance.
The surgeon uses a bipolar grasp to elevate the abdomen, allowing for dissection of the anterior surface of the mass while maintaining the correct anatomical plane.

The surgeon begins lateral dissection of the abdominal mass, applying traction and counter traction to maintain the correct plane. The dissection is performed meticulously, ensuring excellent hemostasis throughout the procedure.
The surgeon successfully removes the entire mass from the abdominal wall, completing the excision process.

The surgeon makes a small skin incision at the site of the previous colostomy closure. A ring forceps is introduced into the abdomen to grasp the mass, which is then extracted and passed off the table as a specimen.
The surgeon identifies a remnant of the abdominal wall mass within the resection cavity and excises it to ensure complete removal of the abdominal wall metastasis.
The surgeon closes the defect in the abdominal wall using a 2-0 barbed suture, taking evenly spaced bites on either side in a continuous running fashion.

The surgeon tightens the suture at the end of the abdominal wall defect and cuts off the needle. A second 2-0 barbed suture is used to complete the two-layered closure in a running continuous fashion.

The surgeon achieves an airtight closure with good approximation of the fascial layers using a two-layered suture technique. Both needles are extracted through the skin incision site at the end of the closure.
The surgeon closes the skin incisions using a running monofilament suture, marking the conclusion of the surgical procedure.
The patient tolerated the procedure well and was recovered in the post-anesthesia care unit. A few hours after surgery, our patient tolerated a regular diet and he was discharged the following day.

He was seen for follow-up two weeks later where his incisions were well healed and he had met all postoperative milestones. He was subsequently referred to the medical oncologist for further adjuvant chemotherapy.
